commit.message:
Restore the mobile graph/reflow section by fixing the note-web module

The mobile homepage graph looked blank because the note-web module was
failing at runtime: it called layoutWithLines without importing it, so
the whole combined graph/reflow section aborted before rendering any
cards. This fix restores the missing import and keeps the combined
homepage interaction alive on both mobile and desktop.

Constraint: The repair had to be minimal and runtime-focused because the deployed behavior was already broken
Constraint: No structural redesign was mixed into the bugfix
Rejected: Rework the mobile layout first | the immediate issue was the module crash, not styling
Rejected: Hide the graph on mobile as a fallback | it would dodge the bug instead of fixing it
Confidence: high
Scope-risk: narrow
Reversibility: clean
Directive: When refactoring shared modules, re-run browser-level checks on the pages that actually exercise the imported APIs; static syntax checks were not enough here
Tested: node --check static/assets/pretext-note-web.mjs; cargo fmt --all; cargo clippy -q --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -q; cargo run -q -p blog-build -- --site-url https://example.test --title 'Jinhyuk Kim — Signals & Notes' --description 'AI research notes, connected writing, and daily news radar.'; local mobile browser screenshot confirming the graph cards now render

commit.message:
Unify the homepage around a graph-driven reflow section

The previous pass still treated the dragon-style reflow and the note web
as separate ideas. This rewrite folds them together into one graph-first
section: the note cards are now the moving obstacles, the explanatory
copy reflows around the graph itself, and the selected-note panel stays
attached to the same interaction.

That makes the homepage feel like one coherent system instead of a stack
of unrelated demos. The cards remain draggable, the text stays readable,
and mobile gets a compact layout strategy with fewer nodes so the reflow
still makes sense on a phone.

Constraint: Existing graph data and note routes stayed the source of truth
Constraint: The combined section had to remain readable on both desktop and mobile
Rejected: Keep the separate dragon block demo above the graph | it split the interaction into two competing ideas
Rejected: Replace the graph with static cards again | would lose the graph-first direction the user asked for
Confidence: high
Scope-risk: moderate
Reversibility: clean
Directive: Treat the homepage intro text and graph as one composition now; future changes should preserve that single-surface interaction rather than reintroducing separate hero demos
Tested: node --check static/assets/pretext-note-web.mjs; cargo fmt --all; cargo clippy -q --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -q; cargo run -q -p blog-build -- --site-url https://example.test --title 'Jinhyuk Kim — Signals & Notes' --description 'AI research notes, connected writing, and daily news radar.'; local agent-browser desktop/mobile screenshots for the unified graph-reflow section

Replace the homepage hero with a Pretext dragon reflow section

after multiple rounds of spacing fixes. This pass removes that approach
entirely and opens the homepage with a dedicated Pretext obstacle-flow
section inspired by the Dragon Reflow demo: text is laid out line by
line around a draggable block so the page feels typographic and alive
without depending on a giant headline.

issue context move into a compact side stack, while the note web and the
article list remain as secondary sections beneath the reflow panel.

Constraint: The interaction needed to stay lightweight enough for mobile as well as desktop

Confidence: medium
Scope-risk: moderate

commit.message:
Move built-in overlay icons next to their owning items

Localize built-in overlay icon assets so metadata no longer depends on a central core sprite for normal UI rendering.

This commit:
- adds item-local SVG icon assets for drawing, connector, shape, BPMN, frame, rich text, dice, screen, deck, and card overlays
- rewires built-in overlay metadata to reference those local assets directly
- preserves dynamic swatch/state hints for fill, stroke, text-size, and tool-color icons
- keeps symbol support in the overlay contract for extensibility, while making current built-ins asset-first
- adds a new UI integration handout that matches the metadata and icon structure now present in core
- marks older handouts as superseded and removes the outdated icon-only handout
- cleans up rebase fallout in the shape and rich-text overlay files

The result is a cleaner plugin boundary: icons now live with the item families that own them, and the UI can render built-in overlays directly from core-provided assets and metadata.

Build verification: bun run build

Co-authored-by: OpenAI Codex <codex@openai.com>

[raster] Clamp reflected gradient LUT coordinates

Malformed reflected gradients can produce very large finite
gradient parameters. The raster backend normalized reflect
mode by casting floorf(t) to int, which can overflow and
drive LUT lookups past the fixed-size stack buffer.

Fold reflected coordinates with fmodf(), clamp LUT inputs
defensively, and add the minimized oss-fuzz raster
testcase to the corpus.

Fixes: https://oss-fuzz.com/testcase-detail/6597066439852032
Tested: meson test -C build
Co-Authored-By: OpenAI Codex <codex@openai.com>

fix(auth): Preserve MCP auth pause after empty aborts (#162)

Preserve MCP auth pauses when the aborted turn only leaves behind an
empty assistant frame.

The auth handoff was sometimes falling through to the generic
execution-failure reply instead of parking the turn for resume. The root
cause was that we treated any non-error trailing assistant frame as a
completed turn, even when Pi had only emitted an empty assistant message
during the OAuth abort path.

This tightens the completion check to require actual assistant text
before suppressing the auth pause. It also adds regression coverage for
the empty-frame abort case and a Slack-facing integration assertion that
resumed MCP auth flows do not post the generic execution-failure
message.

Validated with the focused auth integration coverage, the full
@sentry/junior package test suite, and package typecheck.

Co-authored-by: Codex <codex@openai.com>

chore(deps): consolidate Dependabot Go module updates (#1098)

### Motivation
- Reduce Dependabot noise by rolling up multiple open, passing
Dependabot Go module updates into a single, non-breaking dependency bump
PR.
- Keep the module graph consistent and resolve transitive changes so
local development and CI see the same versions.
- Validate changes using the repository Makefile-native workflow and
sign off with the project DCO policy.

### Description
- Applied the Dependabot-targeted minor/patch bumps and compatible
transitive updates in `go.mod`/`go.sum`, including (not exhaustive)
`github.com/cli/cli/v2` → `v2.89.0`, `github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go` →
`v0.47.0`, `github.com/yuin/goldmark` → `v1.8.2`, `golang.org/x/oauth2`
→ `v0.36.0`, `google.golang.org/api` → `v0.274.0`, `golang.org/x/net` →
`v0.52.0`, `github.com/fatih/color` → `v1.19.0`,
`github.com/go-playground/validator/v10` → `v10.30.2`, and
`go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/net/http/otelhttp` →
`v0.67.0`.
- Updated `go.sum` via dependency resolution and ran `go mod tidy` to
clean the module graph and checksum file.
- Performed dependency upgrades with `go get -t -u ./...` and recorded
the resulting module set changes in `go.mod`/`go.sum`.
- Changes were committed with DCO signoff and are available in the
working branch at commit `ae9bcfefedf486335008047cf8ea787beaea8f1b`.

### Testing
- Ran `go get -t -u ./...` which completed and upgraded direct and
transitive modules successfully.
- Ran `go mod tidy` which completed and normalized `go.mod`/`go.sum`
successfully.
- Ran `make install/dev` which completed (tool install steps ran, though
the environment lacks a git tag description and some tool binaries may
be placed under the container GOPATH).
- Ran `make lint` which failed due to a missing vet tool binary: `go vet
-vettool=/root/go/bin/checklocks ...` could not find
`/root/go/bin/checklocks` in this environment.
- Ran `make test` which failed in this container due to
environment/tooling/runtime expectations (examples include missing
`deno`, interpreters/tooling for some file-command tests producing empty
output, and tests asserting specific version/frontmatter metadata that
differ in this ephemeral environment).

ref: Improve architecture with boundary enforcement and service extraction (#159)

Improve the junior package architecture by splitting oversized
orchestration modules into focused services, extracting isolated build
and tool-execution helpers, and replacing bespoke boundary enforcement
with maintained tooling.

The original motivation was to reduce the fix-chain pattern around
`respond.ts`, `agent-tools.ts`, and `nitro.ts`, where unrelated
responsibilities were coupled closely enough that small changes tended
to cascade. This branch moves post-execution result handling, checkpoint
persistence, and MCP auth pause handling into dedicated chat services;
breaks Nitro build concerns into small `build/` modules; and extracts
tool execution helpers so sandbox input construction, credential
injection, result normalization, and tool error handling are
independently testable.

The branch also tightens the supporting architecture around that
refactor. Chat tool availability now flows through explicit channel
capabilities instead of implicit conversation-scoped checks. GenAI
logging and tracing semantics are aligned with the updated logging
specs, and the empty-response diagnostic path keeps generated file
counts intact. The PR also adds the missing JSDoc on
`generateAssistantReply`, restores lost context comments, and keeps the
architecture and plugin specs in sync with the code.

Since the branch now owns architecture enforcement, it no longer uses
the custom `check-chat-architecture-boundary.mjs` script. Instead,
`@sentry/junior` uses `oxlint` for package linting and
`dependency-cruiser` for chat import boundary rules, with `lint-staged`
running `oxlint --fix` before Prettier on staged JS and TS files. This
is simpler to maintain and catches the relative-import boundary cases
the custom script missed.

A few pre-existing failures and test-shape issues were fixed while
landing the refactor. The MCP progressive loading test now lives at the
integration layer, example build discovery refreshes the pnpm install
after rebuilding the package, the webhook lazy-load test has a less
brittle timeout, and several focused unit tests were rewritten so they
fail deterministically instead of relying on conditional assertions or
unsafe optional chaining.

The result is still one branch, but the current shape is narrower and
more explicit than where it started: smaller module surfaces,
architecture checks backed by standard tooling, and a clean `pnpm
--filter @sentry/junior lint` baseline for follow-on work.

commit.message:
fix(import): handle phone uniqueness conflicts + speed up preview progress (#1093)

* fix(import): handle phone uniqueness conflicts during order commit + speed up preview progress

Two fixes for the import job pipeline:

1. Customer phone dedup (resolve-import-customer.ts): Fix customers_merchant_phone_unique
   constraint violation during order commit. Phone-only orders now link to existing phone
   holders with email/user_id. Email-identified orders INSERT with phone=null when phone
   is already taken. Safety net retries INSERT without phone on concurrent 23505 errors.

2. Preview progress cold-start (import-job-service.ts): Report totalRows to the UI as
   soon as CSV is parsed, before existing orders/products DB queries finish. This closes
   the gap where the UI shows 0/0 while waiting for unrelated DB loads.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix(import): improve retry error message and fix phone cache corruption

- Include original insertError.message as fallback when retryError is null
- Only update customersByPhone cache using the actual inserted phone value,
  not the original order phone (which may have been nulled out to avoid
  the uniqueness constraint)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix(import): tighten phone match guard and handle phone-only concurrent collisions

- Change allPhoneMatches guard from length > 0 to === 1 to avoid ambiguous merges
- For phone-only orders hitting 23505, look up existing phone holder instead of
  inserting a null-phone row that would lose the phone identifier
- Email-identified orders still retry INSERT with phone=null as before

commit.message:
Build IAM user directory governance and deprovision lifecycle

* feat(iam): add user directory governance flows

Summary:
- add user directory domain, repo, service, and HTTP API flows for
  listing users, inspecting identities, and auditable status
  transitions
- harden OIDC sync around issuer+subject identity semantics,
  idempotent updates, and fail-closed email collision handling
- add security settings UI, API client types, generated OpenAPI
  artifacts, docs, and coverage for governance and deprovision flows

Rationale:
- upgrade the existing login-time OIDC cache into an admin-facing user
  directory with explicit lifecycle controls and auditability
- keep identity semantics stable when mutable claims change while
  avoiding unsupported auto-link and merge behavior
- document current support boundaries so manual disable works today and
  webhook or SCIM follow-up work has a clear contract

Tests:
- PATH="$PWD/.tooling/go/bin:$HOME/.local/go1.26.1/bin:$PATH" go test ./internal/repo/humanauth ./internal/service/humanauth ./internal/httpapi
- PATH="$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.1/bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm --dir web exec vitest run src/lib/features/settings/components/security-settings.test.ts
- PATH="$PWD/.tooling/go/bin:$HOME/.local/go1.26.1/bin:$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.1/bin:$PATH" make openapi-check-ci (expected generated diff before commit)

Co-authored-by: Codex <codex@openai.com>

* fix(iam): resolve merge-time validation drift

Summary:
- align the new instance user directory authorization mapping with the
  current security permission keys on main
- split the user directory UI and settings regression tests so the web
  lint budgets pass without changing behavior
- document the new user directory API routes as intentional CLI gaps and
  add domain tests to restore 100 percent core coverage

Rationale:
- the merge from origin/main exposed a renamed security permission that
  broke openapi generation and downstream validation
- the feature landed cleanly, but CI still enforced file-length and
  coverage contracts that needed a follow-up refactor and test pass
- keeping parity expectations explicit avoids hidden CLI drift while the
  user directory stays API-only for now

Tests:

commit.message:
Strip the presentation back to a simpler publication baseline

The interaction web idea is better than the previous decorative passes,
but the surrounding shell still carried too much visual baggage. This
reset removes the leftover kinetic-home scripts and rewrites the global,
note, tag, and graph styles around a quieter baseline so the content and
note-to-note interaction become the primary visual focus.

The result is a lighter, flatter publication system: simple surfaces,
clean borders, calmer navigation, and less ornamental contrast. The home
interaction web remains, while the rest of the site now shares a much
more restrained reading-first style.

Constraint: The homepage note-web behavior had to keep working while the rest of the styling was simplified
Constraint: Existing routes and server-side templates remained the source of truth
Rejected: Keep the dark glossy shell and only tweak colors | still felt over-designed
Rejected: Remove the note web as well | it was the one interaction direction the user explicitly wanted to keep
Confidence: high
Scope-risk: moderate
Reversibility: clean
Directive: Add visual complexity only when it directly improves note discovery or reading; avoid standalone flourishes
Tested: node --check static/assets/pretext-note-web.mjs; cargo fmt --all; cargo clippy -q --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -q; cargo run -q -p blog-build -- --site-url https://example.test --title 'Jinhyuk Kim — Signals & Notes' --description 'AI research notes, connected writing, and daily news radar.'; local agent-browser screenshots for home top, interaction web, and note page; local eval verifying note-web hover updates the detail panel
Not-tested: Production view after this minimal reset push
Co-authored-by: Codex <codex@openai.com>

commit.message:
Center the homepage around a simpler note interaction web

The previous kinetic pass was still too styled and too self-conscious for
what the blog needed. This revision strips the homepage back to a
clearer introduction, a focused note-web panel, and straightforward
secondary lists so the interaction itself does the work instead of a lot
of decorative chrome.

Each markdown note on the landing page is now represented by a
Pretext-sized card inside a local interaction web. Labels are measured
with Pretext, links are drawn from the real graph data, and hovering a
note highlights its neighbors while updating the side detail panel.

Constraint: Existing note routes and graph data had to remain the source of truth
Constraint: The homepage needed to degrade cleanly without changing the markdown pipeline
Rejected: Keep the animated hero as the homepage focal point | it distracted from the notes themselves
Rejected: Fall back to a simple card list on home | it would not deliver the interaction-web behavior the user asked for
Confidence: high
Scope-risk: moderate
Reversibility: clean
Directive: Keep the homepage interaction web legible first; if more motion is added later, do not let it obscure note titles or link structure
Tested: node --check static/assets/pretext-note-web.mjs; cargo fmt --all; cargo clippy -q --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -q; cargo run -q -p blog-build -- --site-url https://example.test --title 'Jinhyuk Kim — Signals & Notes' --description 'AI research notes, connected writing, and daily news radar.'; local agent-browser screenshots of the homepage interaction web
Not-tested: Production interaction-web behavior before the next deploy completes
Co-authored-by: Codex <codex@openai.com>

commit.message:
Turn the blog into a kinetic editorial surface

The previous pass improved structure but still looked like a themed app.
This rework pushes the blog into a much more opinionated publication
shape: dark shell, paper reading surfaces, a stronger hierarchy, and a
homepage cover that behaves like an animated editorial spread instead of
just another content dashboard.

Pretext now does visibly heavier lifting on the landing page. The hero
headline is rendered as measured lines inside a custom stage with moving
orb accents, while the masonry stream keeps using measured copy for
stable card packing. I also added a small reveal layer so archive and
news cards settle into place instead of appearing flatly.

Constraint: Content, routes, and markdown rendering had to stay intact
Constraint: The redesign had to reuse the existing static Rust pipeline
Rejected: Keep iterating on the earlier pastel card design | it still read like a template rather than a publication
Rejected: Animate the entire markdown article body with Pretext | too risky for readability and accessibility
Confidence: medium
Scope-risk: broad
Reversibility: clean
Directive: Keep Pretext concentrated on headline, card, and accent surfaces; if you expand the animated layout further, preserve no-JS fallbacks and readability first
Tested: cargo fmt --all; cargo clippy -q --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings; cargo test -q; cargo run -q -p blog-build -- --site-url https://example.test --title 'Jinhyuk Kim — Signals & Notes' --description 'AI research notes, connected writing, and daily news radar.'; node --check static/assets/pretext-home-hero.mjs; node --check static/assets/pretext-masonry.mjs; node --check static/assets/editorial-motion.js; local agent-browser screenshots for home, note, and tag pages
Not-tested: Production behavior on mud-blog.pages.dev after this second-pass rework push
Co-authored-by: Codex <codex@openai.com>
